diagrams.title: Linked Representation of Binary Tree
diagrams.description: A complete diagram showing how a binary tree is represented using linked nodes with left and right child pointers
elements: Node structure box: [Data | Left Pointer | Right Pointer]
Root node 'A' with left pointer to node B and right pointer to node C
Node B with left pointer to node D and right pointer to node E
Nodes C, D, E with NULL left and right pointers (leaf nodes)
Arrows showing pointer connections between nodes
Memory allocation showing each node as separate memory block
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the node structure with data and two pointer fields, understand how pointers connect parent nodes to child nodes, recognize leaf nodes have NULL pointers, and see how dynamic memory allocation creates non-contiguous storage

diagrams.title: Array Sequential Representation of Binary Tree
diagrams.description: Shows the same binary tree represented sequentially in an array with index-based relationships
elements: Array with index numbers 0 to 4
Array contents: index 0='A', index 1='B', index 2='C', index 3='D', index 4='E'
Index relationships displayed: left child of index i at 2i+1, right child at 2i+2, parent at floor((i-1)/2)
Visual showing how 'B' at index 1 has children D(index 3) and E(index 4) using formulas
Empty slots marked as NULL when tree is incomplete
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the formula-based relationships between parent and child indices, understand how complete binary trees maximize array efficiency, see how array representation uses contiguous memory, and practice calculating child/parent indices using the formulas

diagrams.title: Side-by-Side Comparison: Linked vs Array
diagrams.description: The same binary tree drawn simultaneously in both representations for direct comparison
elements: Left side: Linked representation with node boxes and pointer arrows
Right side: Array representation with indexed boxes
Dotted lines connecting corresponding nodes in both representations
Color coding: root=A(red), level 1=B,C(blue), level 2:D,E(green)
diagrams.purpose: Students visually compare how the same logical tree structure appears in both representations, understand the mapping between pointer links and array indices, and recognize that both represent the same abstract data structure differently

diagrams.title: Incomplete Binary Tree - Array Representation Challenges
diagrams.description: Demonstrates array representation inefficiencies with sparse/incomplete binary trees
elements: An incomplete tree: root A has left child B, B has left child D (missing right child)
Array representation showing NULL entries at indices 2 and 5
Wasted memory spaces highlighted in red
Same tree in linked representation showing only allocated nodes
diagrams.purpose: Students understand when array representation is inefficient (sparse trees), learn to identify wasted space in arrays, and recognize why linked representation is preferred for incomplete trees

diagrams.title: Memory Layout Comparison
diagrams.description: Contrasts contiguous vs non-contiguous memory allocation in array and linked representations
elements: Array representation: single block of contiguous memory cells
Linked representation: scattered memory blocks connected by arrows
Size comparison: array uses n cells, linked uses n nodes + 2n pointer fields
Stack and heap regions labeled in linked representation
diagrams.purpose: Students understand memory allocation differences, learn about cache efficiency advantages of array representation, and see the overhead of storing pointers in linked representation

charts.title: Linked Representation vs Array Representation
charts.content: Comparison table with columns: Aspect, Linked Representation, Array Representation | Rows: Space Complexity, Traversal Ease, Insertion/Deletion, Complete Trees, Sparse Trees, Cache Performance, Implementation Difficulty, Pointer Overhead | Linked: O(n) nodes + O(2n) pointers, Easy (follow pointers), Easy (dynamic), Efficient, Wasteful, Poor (scattered), Moderate, 2n pointers | Array: O(n) for complete, O(2^k) for sparse, Index calculation needed, Complex (shifting), Efficient, Efficient, Easy, No pointers needed

charts.title: Choosing Binary Tree Representation Method
charts.content: Decision flowchart: Is the tree complete or nearly complete? → Yes → Use Array Representation (efficient memory, fast access) | No → Is frequent insertion/deletion required? → Yes → Use Linked Representation (dynamic, no shifting) | No → Consider tree size and application (heaps → array, general trees → linked)

charts.title: Array Index Formulas for Binary Trees
charts.content: Formula table: For node at index i (0-based): Left Child Index = 2i + 1, Right Child Index = 2i + 2, Parent Index = floor((i - 1) / 2), Level of node = floor(log2(i + 1)), Total nodes in complete tree up to level k = 2^(k+1) - 1

charts.title: Binary Tree Representation Methods Classification
charts.content: Classification chart: Binary Tree Representation → Linked/pointer-based (dynamic memory, nodes with left/right pointers, flexible for any tree) → Sequential/Array-based (contiguous memory, index-based access, efficient for complete/sparse trees using formulas) → Specialized (heap implementation, priority queue applications)

branches: Linked Representation (Node Structure: Data + Left Pointer + Right Pointer, Dynamic Memory Allocation, Non-contiguous Storage, Efficient for Sparse Trees, Easy Insertion/Deletion)
Array Representation (Sequential Storage, Contiguous Memory, Index-based Access Formulas, Efficient for Complete Trees, Used in Heaps/Priority Queues)
Index Relationships (Parent = floor((i-1)/2), Left Child = 2i+1, Right Child = 2i+2)
Memory Considerations (Linked: n + 2n overhead, Array: minimal overhead when complete)
Applications (Linked: File systems, Expression trees, Syntax parsers | Array: Heaps, HeapSort, Priority queues)
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview linking both representation methods to their practical applications, helping students understand when to use each method and how they connect to real-world data structures like heaps and file systems
